figures.caption: Table 3: Logical properties satisfied by [−∞,+∞]+ (Table 2), viewed as a quantitative residuated lattice (a softale) with a real-valued order ⊸ : [−∞,+∞]2 ! [−∞,+∞] coinciding with implication.

Table 2: Algebraic structure of the additive reals [−∞,+∞]+. Operations restricted to {−∞,+∞} behave like either conjunction (blue background) or disjunction (red). Positive and negative polarities relate to each other via De Morgan duality.
Table 11: Hyperparameters used for the experiments.

Table 6: Comparison of ∇Jx ∧ yK against ∇max (defined as (1/2, 1/2) on the diagonal) for the STL (ν = 2) and QLL (p = 2) conjunctions. Top row shows discrepancy in magnitude (lower is better), bottom row discrepancy in direction (closer to 1 is better).
Table 1: Comparison of differentiable logics on logical and analytic features.

Table 13: Experimental results for training and verifying models on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and the Dice using different constraints and differentiable logics. In each experiment, the best-performing configuration with respect to the combination of PAcc and CSat is highlighted in boldface.

Table 4: Names and definitions of four properties ϕ that were used in the continuous verification experiments in this paper. Their QLL formalisations are presented in Appendix A.4, except for the Dice one which is simple enough to not require further discussion. Recall the semantics of yi is fθ(x)i, for all labels i, and where x ∈ Bε(x̂).
Table 9: Surface and contour plots with gradients of conjunction and disjunction operators listed in Table 8.
Table 7: An overview of differentiable logics considered in this paper. J−K denotes the mapping of a logical formula into loss. Connectives used in our experiments are highlighted in blue.

Table 8: An overview of differentiable logics considered in this paper. J∧K and J∨K denote the non-linear connectives, and J⊗K and J⊕K denote the linear ones. Connectives used in our experiments are highlighted in blue. Their plots and gradients are shown in Table 9.

Table 5: Selected experimental results for training and verifying models on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and Dice using different constraints and differentiable logics. Boldface indicates the best-performing configuration in each experiment, measured by product of PAcc and CSat. When a model is trained with a choice of ε, it is then attacked (CSec) and verified (CSat) for the same ε.

Table 10: Surface and contour plots with gradients of the STL conjunction operator for ν ∈ {1, 2, 10} and the QLL conjunction operator for p ∈ {1, 2, 10}. Notice the convergence to the plot and gradients of max (first column, first row in Table 9) for both, as expected. Observe also how STL contour lines are slightly non-convex, which leads to gradients which are slightly misaligned to those of max in the central part of the plot, as seen in Table 6.
